parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST use a degausser of sufficient field strength for the coercivity of the media and IT Equipment.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST use a degausser which has been evaluated as capable for the magnetic orientation (longitudinal or perpendicular) of the media.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST comply with product specific directions provided by the manufacturers, along with any provided by the GCSB.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST perform the destruction of media and IT Equipment under the supervision of at least one person cleared to the highest classification of the media or IT Equipment being destroyed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Personnel supervising the destruction of media or IT Equipment MUST:

supervise the handling of the media or IT Equipment to the point of destruction; and
ensure that the destruction is completed successfully.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST perform the destruction of accountable material under the supervision of at least two personnel cleared to the highest classification of the media or IT Equipment being destroyed.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT outsource the supervision and oversight of the destruction of TOP SECRET or NZEO media and IT equipment or other accountable material to a non-government entity or organisation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies outsourcing the destruction of media and IT Equipment to a commercial facility MUST use an approved facility and comply with the procedures and instructions in this Chapter.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD sanitise media and IT Equipment prior to transporting it to an offsite location for destruction.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST declassify all media and IT equipment prior to disposing of it into the public domain.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Media and IT equipment that cannot be effectively sanitised or declassified MUST be destroyed and not released into the public domain.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST document procedures for the disposal of media and IT equipment.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies declassifying media MUST ensure that:

the reclassification of all classified information on the media has been approved by the originator, or the media has been appropriately sanitised or destroyed; and
formal approval is granted before the media is released into the public domain.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST dispose of media in a manner that does not draw undue attention to its previous classification.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Media and IT equipment that has contained NZEO material MUST be sanitised and redeployed or sanitised and destroyed in accordance with the procedures in this chapter.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: For disposal of all NZEO endorsed materials, an approved destruction facility MUST be used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Media and IT equipment that has contained NZEO material MUST NOT be disposed of via e-recyclers or sold to any third party.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where agencies establish their own disposal/destruction facilities, these facilities MUST be approved by the Director-General GCSB.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST use an approved secure disposal/destruction facility for the destruction of media and IT equipment.

parts.name: statement
statement
statement
statement
parts.prose:
Agencies MUST reduce potential vulnerabilities in their SOEs by:

removing unused accounts;
renaming or deleting default accounts; and
replacing default passwords before or during the installation process.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies SHOULD reduce potential vulnerabilities in their SOEs by:

removing unused accounts;
renaming or deleting default accounts; and
replacing default passwords, before or during the installation process.

parts.name: statement
statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use an Approved Cryptographic Algorithm to perform cryptographic checksums for characterisation purposes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD consider characterisations in the context of a BCP or DRP and any related Business Impact Analyses and Risk Assessments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD review all software applications to determine whether they attempt to establish any unauthorised or unplanned external connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If automated outbound connection functionality is included, agencies SHOULD make a business decision to determine whether to permit or deny these connections, including an assessment of the security risks involved in doing so.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If automated outbound connection functionality is included, agencies SHOULD consider the implementation of Data Loss Prevention (DLP) technologies.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD limit information that could be disclosed outside the agency about what software, and software versions are installed on their systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD implement application allow listing as part of the SOE for workstations, servers and any other network device.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST ensure that a system user cannot disable the application allow listing mechanism.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD prevent a system user from running arbitrary executables.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD restrict a system user’s rights in order to permit them to only execute a specific set of predefined executables as required for them to complete their duties.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD ensure that application allow listing does not replace the antivirus and anti-malware software within a system.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD ensure that system administrators are not automatically exempt from application allow list policy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD ensure that the default policy is to deny the execution of software.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD ensure that application allow listing is used in addition to a strong access control list model and the use of limited privilege accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD plan and test application allow listing mechanisms and processes thoroughly prior to implementation.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD restrict the process creation permissions of any executables which are permitted to run by the application allow listing controls.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD validate executable behaviour, in particular process creation, permission changes and access control modifications through examination, testing, monitoring and restriction of the permissions.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Logs from the application allow listing implementation SHOULD include all relevant information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST develop and implement a policy governing appropriate Web usage.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use a Web proxy for all Web browsing activities.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT permit downloading of executable files from external websites unless there is a demonstrable and approved business requirement.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD disable the automatic launching of files downloaded from external websites.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies permitting TLS through their gateways SHOULD implement:

a solution that decrypts and inspects the TLS traffic as per content filtering requirements; or
an allow list specifying the addresses (uniform resource locators) to which encrypted connections are permitted, with all other addresses blocked.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD seek legal advice regarding the inspection of encrypted TLS traffic by their gateways.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD implement allow listing for all HTTP traffic being communicated through their gateways.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using an allow list on their gateways to specify the external addresses, to which encrypted connections are permitted, SHOULD specify allow list addresses by domain name or IP address.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If agencies do not allow list websites they SHOULD deny list websites to prevent access to known malicious websites.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies deny listing websites SHOULD update the deny list on a frequent basis to ensure that it remains effective.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD block client-side active content, such as Java and ActiveX, which are assessed as having a limited business impact.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies SHOULD:

use client-side controls that allow JavaScript on a per website basis; and
add JavaScript functions used only for malicious purposes to the agency Web content filter or IDS/IPS.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use the Web proxy to filter content that is potentially harmful to system users and their workstations.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Users MUST NOT use agency userID and login passwords as credentials for external websites.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Users SHOULD NOT store web site authentication credentials (userID and password) on workstations, remote access devices (such as laptops) or BYO devices.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Users SHOULD NOT use the same password for multiple websites.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies SHOULD ensure that software development environments are configured such that:

there are at least three separate environments covering:

development;
testing; and
production.

information flow between the environments is strictly limited according to a defined and documented change policy, with access granted only to system users with a clear business requirement;
new development and modifications only take place in the development environment; and
write access to the authoritative source for the software (source libraries & production environment) is disabled.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: Software SHOULD be reviewed or tested for vulnerabilities before it is used in a production environment.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Software SHOULD be reviewed or tested by an independent party as well as the developer.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Software development SHOULD follow secure coding practices and agency development standards.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD review all active content on their Web servers for known information security issues.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD minimise connectivity and access between each Web application component.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD follow the documentation provided in the Open Web Application Security Project guide to building secure Web applications and Web services.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST develop and implement a policy governing the use of email.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST ensure that emails containing NZEO or other nationality releasability marked information are sent only to named recipients.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT transmit emails or other documents, containing NZEO or other nationality releasability marks, to groups or distribution lists unless the nationality of all members of the distribution lists can be confirmed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD comply with the national classification system for the application of protective markings.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT allow system users to select protective markings that the system has not been accredited to process, store or communicate.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT allow a protective marking to be inserted into system user generated emails without their intervention.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT permit system users replying to or forwarding an email to select a protective marking that indicates that the classification of the email is lower than a previous classification used for the email.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All classified and unclassified emails MUST have a protective marking.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Email protective markings MUST accurately reflect the highest classification of all elements in an email, including any attachments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD include protective markings in the email subject line or header to facilitate early identification of the classification.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where an unmarked email has originated outside the government, the agency MUST assess the information and determine how it is to be handled in accordance with the classification system.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where an email is of a personal nature and does not contain government information, protective markings SHOULD NOT be used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where an unmarked email has originated from a New Zealand or overseas government agency, personnel SHOULD contact the originator to determine how it is to be handled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where an email is received with an unknown protective marking from a New Zealand or overseas government agency, personnel SHOULD contact the originator to determine appropriate protection measures.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD configure systems so that the protective markings appear at the top and bottom of every page when the email is printed, in CAPITALS and appearing as the first and last item on each page.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Personnel SHOULD NOT send emails that contain active Web addresses or click on active Web addresses within emails they receive.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST make their system users aware of the agency’s email usage policies.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD implement measures to monitor their personnel’s compliance with email usage policies.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD enforce the use of approved government email systems such as SEEMAIL.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT allow personnel to use public Web-based email services, for processing, receiving or sending emails or attachments for official business.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST enable DMARC with a policy of p=reject for all email originating from or received by their domain(s). See 15.2.16 for a recommended approach where a domain is used for sending and/or receiving email and disruption would cause a business impact.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST manage “received DMARC messages” in accordance with the agency’s published DMARC policy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST review DMARC reports on a regular basis and address any identified anomalies or security issues.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD produce failure reports and aggregate reports according to the agency’s DMARC policies.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: Email servers SHOULD be configured to strip active addresses and URL’s and replace them with text only versions.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST prevent unmarked and inappropriately marked emails being sent to intended recipients by blocking the email at the email server, originating workstation or both.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST enforce protective marking of emails so that checking and filtering can take place.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD enforce protective marking of emails so that checking and filtering can take place.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST configure systems to block any outbound emails with a protective marking or endorsement indicating that the content of the email exceeds the classification of the communication path.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD configure systems to log every occurrence of a blocked email.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST configure email systems to reject, log and report inbound emails with protective markings indicating that the content of the email exceeds the accreditation of the receiving system.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD notify the intended recipient of any blocked emails.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD only send notification of undeliverable, bounced, or blocked emails to senders that can be verified via SPF or other trusted means.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST ensure that the requirements for blocking unmarked and outbound emails are also applied to automatically forwarded emails.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST disable open email relaying so that email servers will only relay messages destined for the agency’s domain(s) and those originating from authorised systems or users within that domain.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD perform regular email server auditing, security reviews and vulnerability analysis activities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where an agency has system users that send email from outside the agency’s network, an authenticated and encrypted channel MUST be configured to allow email to be sent via the centralised email gateway.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD route email through a centralised email gateway.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where backup or alternative email gateways are in place, additional email gateways SHOULD be maintained at the same standard as the primary email gateway.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST enable opportunistic TLS encryption as defined in IETF’s RFC 3207 on email servers that make incoming or outgoing email connections over public infrastructure.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD implement TLS between email servers where significant volumes of classified information are passed via email to other agencies.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD enable MTA-STS to prevent the unencrypted transfer of emails between complying servers.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST use TLS 1.2 or above when implementing MTA-STS.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD enable TLS reporting when implementing MTA-STS.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies MUST:

specify mail servers using SPF or Sender ID; and 
mark, block or identify incoming emails that fail SPF checks for notification to the email recipient.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies MUST:

use a fail SPF record when specifying email servers; and 
use SPF or Sender ID to verify the authenticity of incoming emails.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD refer to the SPF recommendations in IETF’s RFC 7208.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST enable DKIM signing on all email originating from their domain.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST use DKIM in conjunction with SPF.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD verify DKIM signatures on emails received, taking into account that email distribution list software typically invalidates DKIM signatures.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where agencies operate email distribution list software used by external senders, agencies SHOULD configure the software so that it does not impair the validity of the sender’s DKIM signature.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD design and implement Zero Trust principles and architecture to strengthen identification management.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies MUST ensure that all system users are:

uniquely identifiable; and
authorised and authenticated on each occasion that access is granted to a system.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT use shared credentials to access accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies MUST NOT use shared credentials to access administrator or privileged access accounts.

NB: Break Glass accounts are exempt from this control. For further guidance on Break Glass accounts see Emergency accounts (Break Glass accounts section).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT use shared credentials to access accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If agencies choose to allow shared, non user-specific accounts they MUST ensure that an independent means of determining the identification of the system user is implemented and logged.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT use a numerical password (or personal identification number) as the sole method of authenticating a system user to access a system.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use multi-factor authentication (MFA) when identifying and authenticating system users.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD assess and determine the risk of centralised access management systems, including SSO, to safely manage integration into systems and when using FIM.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST ensure adequate password policies are implemented and enforced across all systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST implement a password policy enforcing at least annual password changes on systems that have not implemented MFA or passwordless authentication.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies MUST implement a password policy enforcing:

A minimum password length of 16 characters (e.g four words).
Passwords must be long, strong and unique. This means passwords must be a minimum character length, and are a combination of unique random words, characters or numbers.

NB: no explicit complexity requirements are enforced (e.g. numbers or special characters), however passwords must be unique, or random and may include special characters and numbers to achieve this.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose:
Agencies MUST NOT:

allow predictable reset passwords;
store passwords in the clear on the system; and
reuse passwords when resetting multiple accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD consider the use of location-based factors in the authentication process, (e.g., Users must be at an expected location (city, country, IP address) and provide the correct credentials for the authentication to succeed).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When creating password policies, agencies SHOULD consider implementing annual password changes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT allow storage of unprotected authentication information that grants system access, or decrypts an encrypted device, to be located on, or with the system or device, to which the authentication information grants access.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST ensure that system authentication data is protected when in transit on organisation networks or All-of-Government systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Password and other authentication secrets MUST be hashed before storage using an approved cryptographic protocol and algorithm. See Chapter 17 – Cryptography.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Passwords and other authentication secrets MUST be stored securely including being:

salted (32 bits or more),

salts should be unique to each password and should be randomly generated.

hashed (HMAC using SHA-2/3), and
“stretched” (such as PBKDF2 with at least:

600k iterations with internal hash function of HMAC-SHA-256; or
210k iterations with internal hash function of HMAC-SHA-512).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where systems contain NZEO or other nationalities releasability marked or protectively marked information, agencies MUST provide a mechanism that allows system users and processes to identify users who are foreign nationals, including seconded foreign nationals.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using NZEO systems SHOULD ensure that identification includes specific nationality for all foreign nationals, including seconded foreign nationals.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST ensure system users provide sufficient evidence to prove they are the owner of the account when requesting a password reset for their system account or making changes to their multi-factor authenticators.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where passwords are not set by the account holder, agencies MUST use temporary passwords when resetting system user accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT use single factor authentication when changing users’ Multi-Factor Authentication details.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Password managers provide no additional security to the sign-in password. Agencies using password managers MUST ensure sign-in passwords adhere to the password security policies used by the organisation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using password managers SHOULD consider the use of MFA to access the password manager.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When moving to passwordless authentication, agencies SHOULD carry out a risk assessment and evaluate passwordless authentication models to choose authentication mechanisms and factors that best fit the organisation’s security and authentication requirements.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST ensure authentication methods that are susceptible to replay attacks are disabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST disable LAN Manager for password authentication on workstations and servers.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD develop and implement a policy to automatically logout and shutdown workstations after an appropriate time of inactivity. This includes invalidating any session tokens.

parts.name: statement
statement
statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD ensure that repeated account lockouts are investigated before reauthorising access.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD have a logon banner that requires a system user to acknowledge and accept their security responsibilities before access to the system is granted.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD seek legal advice on the exact wording of logon banners.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose:
Agencies MUST follow a defined process for developing an access control list, such as described in the table below.

Stage
Description

1
Establish groups of all system resources based on similar security objectives.

2
Determine the information owner for each group of resources.

3

Obtain agreement from system owners.

4

Establish groups encompassing all system users based on similar functions or security objectives.

5

Determine the group owner or manager for each group of system users.

6

Determine the degree of access to the resource for each system user group, incorporating the principal of least-privilege access.

7

Decide on the level of access for security administration, based on the internal security policy.

8

Identify any classification, protective markings, and releasability indicators (such as NZEO or compartmented information).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST have authorisation of system users enforced by access controls.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST restrict access to compartmented information. Such restriction MUST be enforced by the system.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT allow access to NZEO information from systems and facilities not under the sole control of the government of New Zealand and New Zealand citizens.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Unless a multilateral or bilateral security agreement is in place, agencies SHOULD NOT allow access to classified information from systems and facilities not under the sole control of the government of New Zealand and New Zealand citizens.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT allow foreign nationals, including seconded foreign nationals, to have privileged access to systems that process, store or communicate NZEO information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT allow foreign nationals, including seconded foreign nationals, to have privileged access to systems that process, store or communicate classified information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies involved in frequent transfers of data from another system to their system with a lesser classification SHOULD ensure at least one privileged user has a security clearance level commensurate the classification of the higher system.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST establish a Privileged Access Management (PAM) policy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Within the context of agency operations, the agency’s PAM policy MUST define:

a privileged account; and
privileged access.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST manage privileged accounts in accordance with the agency’s PAM policy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST apply the Principle of Least Privilege when developing and implementing a Privileged Access Management (PAM) policy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST use two-factor or Multi-Factor Authentication to allow access to privileged accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD consider the use of time bound revocation to privileged accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: As part of a Privileged Access Management (PAM) policy, agencies MUST establish and implement a strong approval and authorisation process before any privileged access credentials are issued.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Privileged Access credentials MUST NOT be issued until approval has been formally granted.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST establish robust credential suspension and revocation procedures as part of the agency’s Privileged Access Management (PAM) policy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST investigate any indication of compromise or misuse of systems credentials or accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST create and maintain a comprehensive inventory of privileged accounts and the associated access rights and credentials.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST create, implement and maintain a robust system of continuous discovery, monitoring and review of privileged accounts and the access rights and credentials associated with those accounts.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST protect and limit access to activity and audit logs and records.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST develop and implement a response and remediation policy and procedure as part of an agency’s Privileged Access Management (PAM) policy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST implement a Privileged Access Management (PAM) policy training module as part of the agency’s overall user training and awareness requirement.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST authenticate each remote connection and user prior to permitting access to an agency system.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD authenticate both the remote system user and device during the authentication process.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT allow the use of remote privileged access from an untrusted domain, including logging in as an unprivileged system user and then escalating privileges.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT allow the use of remote privileged access from an untrusted domain, including logging in as an unprivileged system user and then escalating privileges.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD establish VPN connections for all remote access connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use Zero Trust principles alongside the use of VPN connections to enhance the security posture of the organisation. This should include removing the ability for a standard user to disable the VPN connection.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST maintain system management logs for the life of a system.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD determine a policy for the retention of system management logs.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose:
Agencies MUST develop and document logging requirements covering:

the logging facility, including:

log server availability requirements; and
the reliable delivery of log information to the log server;

the list of events associated with a system or software component to be logged; and
event log protection and archival requirements.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose:
Agencies SHOULD log the events listed in the table below for specific software components.

Software component
Events to log

Database

System user access to the database.

 

Attempted access that is denied.

 

Changes to system user roles or database rights.

 

Addition of new system users, especially privileged users.

 

Modifications to the data.

 

Modifications to the format or structure of the database.

Network/operating system

Successful and failed attempts to logon and logoff.

 

Changes to system administrator and system user accounts.

 

Failed attempts to access data and system resources.

 

Attempts to use special privileges.

 

Use of special privileges.

 

System user or group management.

 

Changes to the security policy.

 

Service failures and restarts.

 

System startup and shutdown.

 

Changes to system configuration data.

 

Access to sensitive data and processes.

 
Data import/export operations.

Web application

System user access to the Web application.

 

Attempted access that is denied.

 

System user access to the Web documents.

 

Search engine queries initiated by system users.

parts.name: statement
statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD establish an authoritative time source.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD synchronise all logging and audit trails with the time source to allow accurate time stamping of events.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Event logs MUST be protected from: 

modification;
unauthorised access; and
whole or partial loss within the defined retention period.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST configure systems to save event logs to separate secure servers as soon as possible after each event occurs.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies SHOULD ensure that:

systems are configured to save event logs to a separate secure log server; and
event log data is archived in a manner that maintains its integrity.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs MUST be archived and retained for an appropriate period as determined by the agency.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Disposal or archiving of DNS, proxy, event, systems and other operational logs MUST be in accordance with the provisions of the relevant legislation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD seek advice and determine if their logs are subject to legislation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD retain DNS, proxy and event logs for a minimum of 12 months.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies should prioritise their log retention requirements based on the risks surrounding their most sensitive systems.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD have a monitoring solution implemented that enables detection of incidents as they occur so that appropriate responses can be taken in adequate timeframes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD have systems available for processing system event logs to identify and correlate events which indicate behavioural anomalies or potential security compromise in the systems, in a near real-time manner.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST undertake a risk analysis before designing and implementing MFA.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Where an agency has external facing systems, cloud-based services, or is authenticating to third-party services services, they MUST:

require MFA for all user accounts; and
implement a secure, multi-factor process to allow entities to reset their standard user credentials.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Where an agency has implemented MFA they MUST:

require MFA for administrative or other high privileged users; and
implement a secure, multi-factor process to allow entities to reset their standard user credentials.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST implement MFA on all user accounts with remote access to organisational resources.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD implement MFA on all user accounts with access to organisational resources.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where agencies have implemented MFA, they SHOULD implement phishing-resistant MFA on administration accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use phishing-resistant MFA when authenticating users to systems.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: The design of an organisations MFA system SHOULD be integrated with the agency’s Information Security Policy, the agency’s Privileged Access Management (PAM) Policy, and any additional agency password policies.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When agencies’ implement MFA they MUST ensure users have an understanding of the risks, and include appropriate usage and safeguards for MFA in the organisation’s user training and awareness programmes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using cryptographic functionality within a product to protect the confidentiality, authentication, non-repudiation or integrity of information, MUST ensure that the product has completed a cryptographic evaluation recognised by the GCSB.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Cryptographic products MUST provide a means of data recovery to allow for recovery of data in circumstances where the encryption key is unavailable due to loss, damage or failure.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Cryptographic products SHOULD provide a means of data recovery to allow for recovery of data in circumstances where the encryption key is unavailable due to loss, damage or failure.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Encryption used to reduce storage or physical handling protection requirements MUST be an approved cryptographic algorithm in an EAL2 (or higher) encryption product.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If an agency wishes to reduce the storage or physical transfer requirements for IT equipment or media that contains classified information, they MUST encrypt the classified information using High Assurance Cryptographic Equipment (HACE).  It is important to note that the classification of the information itself remains unchanged.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
If an agency wishes to use encryption to reduce the storage, handling or physical transfer requirements for IT equipment or media that contains classified information, they MUST use:

full disk encryption; or
partial disk encryption where the access control will allow writing ONLY to the encrypted partition holding the classified information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
If an agency wishes to use encryption to reduce the storage or physical transfer requirements for IT equipment or media that contains classified information, they SHOULD use:

full disk encryption; or
partial disk encryption where the access control will allow writing ONLY to the encrypted partition holding the classified information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST use an Approved Cryptographic Algorithm to protect NZEO information when at rest on a system.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST use HACE if they wish to communicate or pass information over UNCLASSIFIED, insecure or unprotected networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Information or systems classified RESTRICTED or SENSITIVE MUST be encrypted with an Approved Cryptographic Algorithm and Protocol if information is transmitted or systems are communicating over insecure or unprotected networks, such as the Internet, public networks or non-agency controlled networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST encrypt aggregated agency data using an approved algorithm and protocol over insecure or unprotected networks such as the Internet, public infrastructure or non-agency controlled networks when the compromise of the aggregated data would present a significant impact to the agency.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD encrypt agency data using an approved algorithm and protocol if they wish to communicate over insecure or unprotected networks such as the Internet, public networks or non-agency controlled networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When IT equipment storing encrypted information is turned on and authenticated, it MUST be treated as per the original classification of the information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST consult the GCSB for further advice on the powered off status and treatment of specific software, systems and IT equipment.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: In addition to any encryption already in place for communication mediums, agencies MUST use an Approved Cryptographic Protocol and Algorithm to protect NZEO information when in transit.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD establish cryptoperiods for all keys and cryptographic implementations in their systems and operations.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use risk assessment techniques and guidance to establish cryptoperiods.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using HACE MUST consult the GCSB for key management requirements.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST ensure that only Approved Cryptographic Algorithms can be used when using an unevaluated product that implements a combination of approved and non-approved Cryptographic Algorithms.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use ECDH and ECDSA for all new systems, version upgrades and major system modifications.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using DH, for the approved use of agreeing on encryption session keys, MUST use a modulus of at least 3072 bits.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Devices which are NOT capable of implementing required key lengths MUST be reconfigured with the longest feasible key length as a matter of urgency.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Devices which are NOT capable of implementing required key lengths MUST be scheduled for replacement as a matter of urgency.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using DSA, for the approved use of digital signatures, MUST use a modulus of at least 1024 bits.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using ECDH, for the approved use of agreeing on encryption session keys, MUST implement the curve P-384 (prime moduli).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All VPN’s using an ECDH key length less than 384 MUST replace all Pre-Shared Keys with keys of at least 384 bits, as soon as possible.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using ECDSA, for the approved use of digital signatures, MUST implement the curve P-384 (prime moduli).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using RSA, for the approved use of digital signatures and passing encryption session keys or similar keys, MUST use a modulus of at least 3072 bits.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using RSA, for the approved use of digital signatures and passing encryption session keys or similar keys, MUST ensure that the public keys used for passing encrypted session keys are different to the keys used for digital signatures.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using RSA, for the approved use of digital signatures and passing encryption session keys or similar keys, SHOULD use a modulus of at least 4096 bits.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using RSA keys within internet X.509 Public Key Infrastructure certificates MUST use a modulus of at least 2048 bits.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST use the SHA-2 family for new systems. Use of SHA-1 is permitted ONLY for legacy systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST use a minimum of SHA-384 when using hashing algorithms to provide integrity protection for information classified as RESTRICTED/SENSITIVE or above.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: In all other cases when information requires integrity protection using hashing algorithms, Agencies MUST use a minimum of SHA-256.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Memorised secrets such as passwords MUST be stored in a form that is resistant to offline attacks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Memorised secrets such as passwords SHOULD be salted and hashed using a suitable one-way key derivation function. See 17.2.14.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The salt SHOULD be at least 32 bits in length, be chosen arbitrarily, and each instance is unique so as to minimise salt value collisions among stored hashes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using approved symmetric encryption algorithms (e.g. AES) SHOULD NOT use Electronic Code Book (ECB) mode.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using a product that implements an Approved Cryptographic Protocol MUST ensure that only Approved Cryptographic Protocols can be used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use the current version of TLS (version 1.3).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT use any version of SSL.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
The table below outlines the settings that SHOULD be implemented when using SSH.

Configuration description

Configuration directive

Disallow the use of SSH version 1

Protocol 2

On machines with multiple interfaces, configure the SSH daemon to listen only on the required interfaces

ListenAddress 
xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx

Disable connection forwarding

AllowTCPForwarding no

Disable gateway ports

Gatewayports no

Disable the ability to login directly as root

PermitRootLogin no

Disable host-based authentication

HostbasedAuthentication no

Disable rhosts-based authentication

RhostsAuthentication no
IgnoreRhosts yes

Do not allow empty passwords

PermitEmptyPasswords no

Configure a suitable login banner
Banner/directory/filename

Configure a login authentication timeout of no more than 60 seconds
LoginGraceTime xx

Disable X forwarding 
X11Forwarding no

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use public key-based authentication before using password-based authentication.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies that allow password authentication SHOULD use techniques to block brute force attacks against the password.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use parameter checking when using the ‘forced command’ option.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: Agencies that use remote access without the use of a password SHOULD use the ‘forced command’ option to specify what command is executed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies that use SSH-agent or other similar key caching programs SHOULD:

only use the software on workstation and servers with screenlocks;
ensure that the key cache expires within four hours of inactivity; and
ensure that agent credential forwarding is used when multiple SSH traversal is needed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD ensure that the latest implementation of SSH software is being used. Older versions contain known vulnerabilities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Decommissioning of faulty or redundant equipment MUST comply with media sanitisation requirements described in Chapter 12 – Product Security.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT allow versions of S/MIME earlier than 3.0 to be used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST immediately revoke key pairs when a private certificate is suspected of being compromised or leaves the control of the agency.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use tunnel mode for IPSec connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies choosing to use transport mode SHOULD additionally use an IP tunnel for IPSec connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use the ESP protocol for IPSec connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using ISAKMP SHOULD disable aggressive mode for IKE.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use a security association lifetime of four hours or 14400 seconds, or less.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use HMAC-SHA256, HMAC-SHA384 or HMAC-SHA512 as the HMAC algorithm.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use the largest modulus size available for the DH exchange.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use Perfect Forward Secrecy for IPSec connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD disable the use of XAUTH for IPSec connections using IKEv1.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD assess the risks associated around key ownership, possession, and control against their own security and business requirements.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD develop a KMP when they have implemented a cryptographic system using commercial grade cryptographic equipment.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The level of detail included in a KMP MUST be consistent with the criticality and classification of the information to be protected.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
The table below describes the minimum contents which SHOULD be documented in the KMP.

Topic 
 Content

Objectives

Objectives of the cryptographic system and KMP, including organisational aims.
Refer to relevant NZCSIs.

System description

The environment.
Maximum classification of information protected.
Topology diagram(s) and description of the cryptographic system topology including data flows.
The use of keys.
Key algorithm.
Key length.
Key lifetime.

Roles and administrative responsibilities

Documents roles and responsibilities, including, if relevant, the COMSEC custodian, cryptographic systems administrator, record keeper, cloud service provider, and auditor.

Accounting

How accounting will be undertaken for the cryptographic system.
What records will be maintained.
How records will be audited.

Classification

Classification of the cryptographic system hardware.
Classification of cryptographic system software.
Classification of the cryptographic system documentation.

Information security incidents

A description of the conditions under which compromise of key material should be declared.
References to procedures to be followed when reporting and dealing with information security incidents.

Key management

Who generates keys.
How keys are delivered.
How keys are received.
Key distribution, including local, remote and central.
How keys are installed.
How keys are transferred.
How keys are stored.
How keys are recovered.
How keys are revoked.
How keys are destroyed.
Each time key information or material is accessed, details are captured in logs.
Approved access lists to cryptographic keys. 

Maintenance

Maintaining the cryptographic system software and hardware.
Destroying equipment and media.

References

Vendor documentation.
Related policies.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST be able to readily account for all transactions relating to cryptographic system material including identifying hardware and all software versions issued with the equipment and materials, including date and place of issue.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD be able to readily account for all transactions relating to cryptographic system material including identifying hardware and all software versions issued with the equipment and materials, including date and place of issue.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST conduct audits using two personnel with cryptographic system administrator access.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies SHOULD conduct audits of cryptographic system material:

on handover/takeover of administrative responsibility for the cryptographic system;
on change of personnel with access to the cryptographic system; and
at least annually.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies SHOULD perform audits to:

account for all cryptographic system material; and
confirm that agreed security measures documented in the KMP are being followed.

parts.name: statement
statement
statement
parts.prose: Cryptographic system equipment SHOULD be stored in a room that meets the requirements for a server room of an appropriate level based on the classification of information the cryptographic system processes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Areas in which cryptographic system material is used SHOULD be separated from other areas and designated as a controlled cryptography area.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST comply with NZCSI when using HACE.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Unkeyed commercial grade cryptographic equipment MUST be distributed and managed by a means approved for the transportation and management of government property.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Keyed commercial grade cryptographic equipment MUST be distributed, managed and stored by a means approved for the transportation and management of government property based on the classification of the key within the equipment.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT transport commercial grade cryptographic equipment or products in a keyed state.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST consider the use of HSMs when undertaking a security risk assessment or designing network and security architectures.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST follow the product selection guidance in this manual. See Chapter 12 – Product Security.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD consider the use of HSMs when undertaking a security risk assessment or designing network and security architectures.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD follow the product selection guidance in this manual. See Chapter 12 – Product Security.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST perform a security risk assessment before providing network documentation to a third party, such as a commercial provider or contractor.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Systems documentation and detailed network diagrams MUST be classified at least to the level of classification of the data to be carried on those systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network documentation provided to a third party, such as to a commercial provider or contractor, MUST contain only the information necessary for them to undertake their contractual services and functions, consistent with the need-to-know principle.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Detailed network configuration information MUST NOT be published in tender documentation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Security aspects SHOULD be considered when determining the classification level of systems and network documentation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD keep the network configuration under the control of a network management authority.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All changes to the configuration SHOULD be documented and approved through a formal change control process.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD regularly review their network configuration to ensure that it conforms to the documented network configuration.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD deploy an automated tool that compares the running configuration of network devices against the documented configuration.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
For each network an agency manages they MUST have:

a high-level diagram showing all connections and gateways into the network; and
a network diagram showing all communications equipment.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
An agency’s network diagrams MUST:

be updated as network changes are made; and
include a ‘Current as at [date]’ statement on each page.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
An agency’s network diagrams SHOULD:

be updated as network changes are made; and
include a ‘Current as at [date]’ statement on each page.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST implement network access controls on all networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD implement network access controls on all networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD implement protection measures to minimise the risk of unauthorised access to network management traffic on a network.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT use SNMP unless a specific requirement exists.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD implement SNMPv3 where a specific SNMP requirement exists.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD change all default community strings in SNMP implementations.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: SNMP access SHOULD be configured as read-only.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Devices MUST NOT be configured to remember and automatically connect to any wireless networks that they have previously connected to.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Wireless auto-connect functionality on devices SHOULD be disabled, preferably by a hardware switch, whenever connected to a fixed network.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies deploying a wireless network for public access MUST separate it from any other agency networks; including BYOD networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT use wireless networks unless the security of the agency’s wireless deployment has been approved by GCSB.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All wireless access points used for government wireless networks MUST be Wi-Fi Alliance certified.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EAP-TLS or PEAP-EAP-TLS MUST be used on wireless networks to perform mutual authentication.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: EAP-TLS, PEAP-EAP-TLS, EAP-TTLS or PEAP MUST be used on wireless networks to perform mutual authentication.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Supplicants, authenticators and the authentication server used in wireless networks MUST have completed an appropriate product evaluation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Supplicants, authenticators and the authentication server used in wireless networks SHOULD have completed an appropriate product evaluation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST generate certificates using a certificate authority product or hardware security module that has completed an appropriate product evaluation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The certificates for both a device and user accessing a wireless network MUST NOT be stored on the same device.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use unique certificates for both devices and users accessing a wireless network.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Certificates for users accessing wireless networks SHOULD be issued on smart cards with access PINs and not stored with a device when not in use.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Certificates stored on devices accessing wireless networks SHOULD be protected by implementing full disk encryption on the devices.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Devices MUST be configured to validate the server certificate, disable any trust for certificates generated by commercial certificate authorities that are not trusted and disable the ability to prompt users to authorise new servers or commercial certification authorities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Devices SHOULD be set to enable identity privacy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The PMK caching period SHOULD NOT be set to greater than 1440 minutes (24 hours).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Communications between wireless access points and a RADIUS server MUST be encapsulated with an additional layer of encryption using an approved encryption product (See Chapter 17 – Cryptography).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using wireless networks MUST ensure that classified information is protected by cryptography that meets the assurance level mandated for the communication of information over unclassified network infrastructure (See Section 17.2, Suite B).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CCMP MUST be used to protect the confidentiality and integrity of all wireless network traffic.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: TKIP and WEP support MUST be disabled or removed from wireless access points.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT use WEP for wireless deployments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT use Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA) for wireless deployments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT use Wi-Fi Protected Access 2 (WPA2) for wireless deployments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use Wi-Fi Protected Access 3 (WPA3) for wireless deployments with preference given to WPA3-Enterprise 192-bit mode.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT use pre-shared keys for wireless authentication.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If pre-shared keys are used, agencies SHOULD use random keys of the maximum allowable length.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT use pre-shared keys for wireless authentication.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD disable the administrative interface on wireless access points for wireless connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Wireless access points and devices SHOULD be upgraded to support a minimum of the 802.11w amendment.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST change the default SSID of wireless access points.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST rename or remove default accounts and passwords.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The SSID of a wireless network SHOULD NOT be readily associated with an agency, the premises, location or the functionality of the network.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT disable SSID broadcasting on wireless networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use the Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) for assigning IP addresses on wireless networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: MAC address filtering SHOULD NOT be used as a security mechanism to restrict which devices connect to a wireless network.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Key generation, distribution and rekeying procedures SHOULD be documented in the SecPlan for the wireless network.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Wireless device drivers and their versions SHOULD be documented in the SecPlan for the wireless network.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where BYOD has been approved by an agency, any wireless network allowing BYOD connections MUST be segregated from all other agency networks, including any agency wireless networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Any BYOD devices MUST comply with the policies and configuration described in Section 21.4– BYOD.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT allow non-agency devices to connect to agency controlled wireless networks not intended or configured for BYOD devices or for public access.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT allow agency devices to connect to non-agency controlled wireless networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Connections between wireless networks and fixed networks SHOULD be treated in the same way as connections between fixed networks and the Internet.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Instead of deploying a small number of wireless access points that broadcast on high power, more wireless access points that use minimal broadcast power SHOULD be deployed to achieve the desired wireless network footprint.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
The effective range of wireless communications outside an agency’s area of control SHOULD be limited by:

Minimising the output power level of wireless devices.
Implementing RF shielding within buildings in which wireless networks are used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Wireless networks SHOULD use channel separation.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use a video, unified communication or voice-aware firewall that meets the same minimum level of assurance as specified for normal firewalls.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD protect VTC and IPT signalling and data by using encryption.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: An encrypted and non-replayable two-way authentication scheme SHOULD be used for call authentication and authorisation.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD ensure that VTC and IPT functions are established using only the secure signalling and data protocols.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST either separate or segregate the VTC and IPT traffic from other data traffic.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD either separate or segregate the IPT traffic from other data traffic.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Authentication and authorisation SHOULD be used for all actions on the IPT network, including:

call setup;
changing settings; and
checking voice mail.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: An encrypted and non-replayable two-way authentication scheme SHOULD be used for call authentication and authorisation.

parts.name: statement

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST NOT connect workstations to VTC or IPT devices unless the workstation or the device, as appropriate for the configuration, uses VLANs or similar mechanisms to maintain separation between VTC, IPT and other data traffic.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT connect workstations to VTC or IPT devices unless the workstation or the device, as appropriate for the configuration, uses VLANs or similar mechanisms to maintain separation between VTC, IPT and other data traffic.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Where an agency uses a VoIP phone in a lobby or shared area they SHOULD limit or disable the phone’s:

ability to access data networks; 
functionality for voice mail and directory services; and
use a separate network segment.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD, where available, use traditional analogue phones in a lobby and shared areas.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using softphones or webcams SHOULD have separate dedicated network interface cards on the host for VTC or IPT network access to facilitate VLAN separation.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using softphones or webcams SHOULD install a host-based firewall on workstations utilising softphones or webcams that allows traffic only to and from a minimum number of ports.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD NOT use softphones or webcams.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD use access control software to control USB ports on workstations using softphones and webcams by utilising the specific vendor and product identifier of the authorised device.

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

parts.name: statement

controls.title: Video & Telephony Conferencing and Internet Protocol Telephony

statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD ensure sufficient resources are provided for the maintenance and monitoring of IDS/IPS.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD deploy IDS/IPSs in all gateways between the agency’s networks and unsecure public networks or BYOD wireless networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD deploy IDS/IPSs at all gateways between the agency’s networks and any network not managed by the agency.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD locate IDS/IPSs within the gateway environment, immediately inside the outermost firewall.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies MUST select IDS / IPS that monitor uncharacteristic and suspicious activities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When signature-based intrusion detection is used, agencies MUST keep the signatures and system patching up to date.

parts.name: statement
statement
parts.prose: In addition to agency defined configuration requirements, agencies SHOULD ensure that IDS/IPSs located inside a firewall are configured to generate a log entry, and an alert, for any information flows that contravene any rule within the firewall rule set.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD test IDS/IPSs rule sets prior to implementation to ensure that they perform as expected.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If a firewall is configured to block all traffic on a particular range of port numbers, the IDP/IPSs SHOULD inspect traffic for these port numbers and generate an alert if they are detected.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies SHOULD deploy tools for:

the management and archive of security event information; and
the correlation of suspicious events or events of interest across all agency networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD install host-based IDS/IPSs on authentication, DNS, email, Web and other high value servers.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Agencies SHOULD use:

filters to block unwanted content and exploits against applications that cannot be patched;
settings within the applications to disable unwanted functionality; and
digital signatures to restrict active content to trusted sources only.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies not using IPv6, but which have deployed dual-stack network devices and ICT equipment that supports IPv6, MUST disable the IPv6 functionality, unless that functionality is required.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network security devices on IPv6 or dual-stack networks MUST be IPv6 capable.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies using IPv6 MUST conduct a security risk assessment on risks that could be introduced as a result of running a dual stack environment or transitioning completely to IPv6.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies implementing a dual stack or wholly IPv6 network or environment MUST re-accredit their networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: IPv6 tunnelling MUST be disabled on all network devices, unless explicitly required.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Dynamically assigned IPv6 addresses SHOULD be configured with DHCPv6 in a stateful manner and with lease information logged and logs stored in a centralised logging facility.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Any network defence elements and devices MUST be IPv6 aware.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: New network devices, including firewalls, IDS and IPS, MUST be IPv6 capable.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies SHOULD consider the use of DNSSEC.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: IPv6 tunnelling MUST be blocked by network security devices at externally connected network boundaries.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies deploying IPv6 equipment in their gateway but not enabling the functionality SHOULD undergo reaccreditation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Agencies enabling a dual-stack environment or a wholly IPv6 environment in their gateways MUST reaccredit their gateway systems.
